course_info.title: Virtual Reality and Augmented Reality
course_info.meeting_schedule: Monday, Wednesday, Friday - 4 hours per week

learning_objectives: Survey the current spectrum of VR and AR hardware platforms
Understand the rendering requirements that distinguish XR from traditional 3D
Compare standalone, tethered, and passthrough VR/AR architectures
Evaluate hardware selection criteria for different XR application types
readings: Meta Quest Developer Documentation - Getting Started

lecture_notes.introduction: Virtual and augmented reality represent a fundamental shift in how humans interact with digital content. Instead of looking at a screen, the user is inside the experience. This changes everything about how we design, render, and think about interactive applications. The VR/AR hardware landscape has matured rapidly: standalone headsets like the Meta Quest 3 deliver six-degree-of-freedom tracking without external sensors, Apple Vision Pro introduces spatial computing with high-fidelity mixed reality, and enterprise headsets like HoloLens 2 serve industrial applications. Each device class presents different capabilities, constraints, and development paradigms. The rendering requirements are also fundamentally different from traditional 3D: you must render the scene twice (once per eye) at 72-120Hz with under 20ms total frame time, or the user experiences discomfort or motion sickness. There is no acceptable frame drop in VR -- every missed frame is a comfort violation that the user perceives viscerally. Today we survey the hardware landscape, examine the stereoscopic rendering pipeline, compare display technologies (LCD, OLED, micro-OLED, pancake lenses), and discuss the tracking systems that enable positional and rotational head tracking. By the end of this session, you should understand the technical specifications that matter for XR development and be able to evaluate which hardware platform suits a given application.
key_concepts: VR headset categories: standalone (Quest 3), tethered PC-VR (Valve Index), console VR (PSVR2), spatial computing (Vision Pro)
AR device spectrum: phone-based AR (ARKit/ARCore), optical see-through (HoloLens 2), video passthrough (Quest 3 MR)
Stereoscopic rendering: rendering from two slightly offset eye positions to create binocular depth perception
Display technologies: LCD (bright, fast), OLED (deep blacks, pentile), micro-OLED (high density, used in Vision Pro)
Lens technologies: fresnel (light, cheap, god rays), pancake (thin, good clarity, light loss), aspheric (heavy, excellent clarity)
Tracking systems: inside-out (camera-based SLAM), outside-in (base stations), hybrid (cameras + IMU)
Degrees of freedom: 3DoF (rotation only) vs 6DoF (rotation + position), controller tracking vs hand tracking
Refresh rate requirements: 72Hz (Quest minimum), 90Hz (standard), 120Hz (premium), and why higher is better for comfort
Field of view: horizontal FOV (90-120 degrees typical), vertical FOV, and the impact on immersion and comfort
IPD adjustment: inter-pupillary distance matching between physical lenses and virtual cameras for correct stereo perception
lecture_notes.demonstration: Setting up a Unity XR project targeting Meta Quest 3: installing XR Plugin Management, configuring the Oculus XR Plugin, setting up the XR Interaction Toolkit, deploying a basic scene to the headset via USB, and verifying head tracking, passthrough, and controller input are all functional. Examining the stereoscopic camera rig and measuring the baseline frame time.
setup_requirements: Unity 2023.2 LTS with XR Plugin Management installed
Meta Quest 3 or Quest Pro headset in developer mode
Meta XR SDK and XR Interaction Toolkit packages installed
USB-C cable for deployment and wireless ADB configured for convenience

review.value: Rendering the scene twice from offset eye positions (typically 63mm apart, matching average human IPD) to create depth perception. Each eye gets a separate render target corrected for lens distortion before display.

Headset cameras observe the environment to determine position and orientation without external sensors. Uses visual-inertial odometry combining camera feature tracking with IMU data for low-latency, drift-resistant tracking.

6DoF tracks both rotation (pitch, yaw, roll) and position (x, y, z), allowing the user to lean, duck, and walk. 3DoF tracks only rotation. All modern VR headsets provide 6DoF for the headset and controllers.

At 90Hz, each frame must complete in 11.1ms. This includes rendering both eyes, submitting to the compositor, and leaving headroom for late-stage reprojection. Exceeding the budget causes judder that rapidly induces nausea.

class_schedule.topic: XR Development Setup with Unity XR
learning_objectives: Configure the Unity XR development pipeline for Meta Quest hardware
Understand the XR Interaction Toolkit architecture and component model
Implement basic head-tracked rendering and controller input
Deploy, debug, and profile XR applications on headset hardware

lecture_notes.introduction: Before we can build compelling VR experiences, we need a solid development pipeline. XR development has unique workflow challenges: you are building for hardware that sits on someone's face, the editor experience cannot fully substitute for headset testing, and debugging requires specialized tools because you cannot see the screen while wearing the headset. Unity's XR ecosystem consists of several interconnected packages: XR Plugin Management handles the abstraction layer between Unity and device-specific SDKs, the XR Interaction Toolkit provides the interaction framework (grab, teleport, UI interaction), and device-specific plugins (Oculus XR Plugin, OpenXR) provide the hardware interface. Today we set up a complete XR development environment: configuring the project for Meta Quest, understanding the XR Origin (the player's root transform in VR space), setting up controller bindings using the Input System, implementing basic interactions (grab, poke, ray), and establishing a deployment workflow that minimizes iteration time. We also set up remote profiling so we can analyze frame timing, draw calls, and GPU utilization while the app runs on the headset. The development loop in VR is longer than traditional development because every change requires a build-and-deploy cycle, so we will discuss strategies for minimizing this overhead including Link/AirLink for tethered testing and scene hot-reloading.
key_concepts: XR Origin: the root transform representing the player's physical space in the virtual world, with camera offset for floor vs eye level
XR Interaction Toolkit architecture: Interactors (controllers/hands), Interactables (objects), Interaction Manager (mediator)
Controller bindings: mapping physical buttons (trigger, grip, thumbstick, A/B/X/Y) to Input System actions
XR Ray Interactor: projecting a ray from the controller for distant object selection and UI interaction
XR Direct Interactor: near-field grab interaction using controller or hand position for picking up objects
Tracked Pose Driver: syncing Unity transforms with headset and controller tracked positions and rotations
Quest Link and Air Link: testing VR scenes in the editor with the headset connected via USB or WiFi
Android Logcat for Quest: reading debug logs from the headset for crash diagnosis and debug output
OVR Metrics Tool: Meta's real-time performance overlay showing FPS, GPU/CPU utilization, and thermal state
Iterative development workflow: editor testing with XR Device Simulator, Link testing for visual checks, on-device builds for final verification
lecture_notes.demonstration: Setting up a complete XR interaction scene: XR Origin with standing-height camera offset, left and right XR Controllers with Ray Interactors for distant selection and Direct Interactors for near grab, a set of Grab Interactable cubes, and a world-space UI canvas with XR UI Input Module for pointer-based menu interaction. Deploying to Quest via USB, verifying all interactions, and running OVR Metrics Tool overlay to check frame timing.
setup_requirements: Unity XR project from Week 1 with XR Interaction Toolkit configured
Meta Quest headset connected via USB-C for deployment
Input System package with XR controller action maps
OVR Metrics Tool installed on the Quest headset

review.value: The transform hierarchy representing the player's tracked space. Camera Offset child contains the Main Camera (tracked to headset position). The XR Origin's world position determines where the player's floor level maps to in the virtual scene.

Interactors (on controllers/hands) detect Interactables (on world objects) and manage hover, select, and activate states. The Interaction Manager ensures only one interactor can select an object at a time and handles event routing.

A Quest system overlay that displays real-time FPS, CPU and GPU frame times, thermal state, and memory usage directly in the headset view. Essential for verifying performance meets requirements during on-device testing.

A Unity Editor tool that simulates headset and controller input using keyboard and mouse, allowing basic XR interaction testing without wearing a headset. Useful for rapid iteration on interaction logic, not for visual or comfort testing.

learning_objectives: Design interaction systems for 3D spatial input in VR environments
Implement direct and indirect manipulation paradigms for different use cases
Create interaction affordances that communicate interactability without tutorials
Handle interaction edge cases: object overlap, hand-through-wall, and release prediction
readings: 3D User Interfaces: Theory and Practice Ch. 5-8
Unity XR Interaction Toolkit - Interactables Guide

lecture_notes.introduction: In VR, interaction is spatial. Unlike a 2D screen where the mouse provides a universal pointing device, VR interaction happens in three-dimensional space with tracked controllers or bare hands. This opens extraordinary design possibilities -- the player can reach out and touch objects, grab them with a natural pinch, throw them with physical velocity, and manipulate them with two-handed gestures -- but it also introduces design challenges that do not exist in traditional gaming. How does the player know which objects can be interacted with? On a 2D screen, a hover state and cursor change indicate interactability. In VR, there is no cursor. You need visual affordances: objects that glow on proximity, outlines that appear when the hand is in grab range, physics-based highlights that respond to nearby controllers. How do you handle the player reaching through a solid wall? In VR, the tracked hand can go anywhere, including inside solid geometry. You need to decide whether the virtual hand stops at the wall (breaking hand-tracking alignment) or passes through (breaking immersion). Today we implement a comprehensive interaction system with both direct manipulation (near-field grab, push, poke) and indirect manipulation (far-field ray pointer), create rich visual affordances using highlight shaders and proximity detection, and solve the common edge cases of VR interaction design.
key_concepts: Direct manipulation: reaching out and physically grabbing, pushing, or poking virtual objects within arm's reach
Indirect manipulation: using a ray or pointer to interact with objects beyond arm's reach
Interaction affordances: visual cues that communicate interactability (glow on proximity, outline on hover, size pulse on valid target)
Grab mechanics: attach point selection (nearest point vs predefined handle), grab offset (maintain relative hand position), release velocity calculation
Two-handed interaction: scaling objects with two grabs, rotating with bimanual manipulation, tool-using gestures
Haptic feedback: controller vibration patterns for grab confirmation, collision, button press, and error states
Interaction priority: resolving conflicts when multiple interactables overlap in grab range
Socket interactions: snap-to-target placement for puzzle pieces, inventory slots, and tool holders
Physics-based interaction: interactable objects with rigidbodies that respond to gravity and collision when not held
Accessibility in interaction: adjustable grab radius, sticky grab option, alternative input methods for users with limited mobility
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building an interactive VR workbench: grabbable tools (hammer, screwdriver) with defined grip points, a physics-based nail that responds to hammer impact, a socket system for returning tools to their holders, two-handed object scaling for examining small parts, ray-based interaction for a wall-mounted instruction panel, and haptic feedback patterns for each interaction type. All objects have proximity-based glow affordances.
setup_requirements: Unity XR project with XR Interaction Toolkit from Week 2
3D models for tools, workbench, and small parts
Highlight shader for proximity-based glow effect
Haptic feedback profiles configured for each interaction type

review.value: The point on an object where the controller or hand attaches when grabbed. Predefined handles ensure the player always grabs a tool by the handle. Dynamic attach preserves the grab offset for more natural manipulation of arbitrary objects.

When the player releases a grabbed object, its velocity is set based on the controller's tracked velocity at release. Averaging velocity over the last 3-5 frames prevents erratic throws from tracking noise on the release frame.

A target location that attracts and snaps interactable objects into position. When the player brings an object near a compatible socket, it smoothly interpolates to the socket position and locks in place. Used for inventory, puzzles, and tool storage.

Controller vibration triggered on interaction events. Different amplitude and duration patterns communicate different events: short pulse for button click, sustained low vibration for grab, sharp burst for collision impact.

learning_objectives: Implement hand tracking with gesture recognition for controller-free interaction
Design input systems that seamlessly transition between hand tracking and controller input
Build custom gesture recognizers for pinch, grab, point, and application-specific gestures
Handle hand tracking limitations: occlusion, range, and reliability
readings: Designing for Hand Tracking (Meta Developer Blog)

lecture_notes.introduction: Hand tracking enables the most natural form of VR interaction: using your actual hands. The Meta Quest cameras track 26 joints per hand in real time, providing position and rotation data for every finger segment. This enables gestures that would be impossible with controllers: precise finger pointing, expressive hand poses, and natural grasping with individual finger articulation. However, hand tracking also introduces significant challenges. Tracking reliability depends on lighting conditions and hand visibility to cameras. Hands can occlude each other, causing tracking loss. There is no haptic feedback when grabbing -- the player feels nothing physical, which creates a disconnect. And the tracking latency (20-30ms) is higher than controller tracking, making fast interactions feel slightly delayed. Today we implement a complete hand tracking system: skeletal hand visualization using tracked joint data, pinch and grab gesture recognition using thumb-finger distance thresholds, pointing gesture detection for ray-based interaction, and a seamless fallback to controller input when hands leave the tracking volume. We also design custom gestures specific to our application and handle the graceful degradation when tracking quality drops.
key_concepts: Hand skeleton data: 26 joints per hand (wrist, palm, thumb x4, index x5, middle x5, ring x5, pinky x5) with position and rotation
Pinch gesture recognition: measuring distance between thumb tip and index tip, with threshold for pinch-start and pinch-end hysteresis
Grab gesture detection: monitoring finger curl angles to detect a closed fist vs open hand
Point gesture: detecting extended index finger with other fingers curled, for ray-based pointing
Custom gesture design: defining application-specific hand poses using joint angle thresholds and relative positions
Hand-controller transitions: detecting when the player picks up or puts down controllers and switching input mode automatically
Tracking confidence: the SDK reports per-hand tracking confidence (high, low); low confidence should trigger visual feedback or fallback
Hand mesh visualization: rendering a mesh or skeleton representation of the tracked hands for visual feedback
Pinch distance vs pinch strength: using normalized 0-1 values for analog control (drawing bow, adjusting dial) vs binary state for selection
Occlusion handling: when one hand occludes the other from cameras, tracking quality degrades; design interactions to avoid both hands in the same camera view
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implementing a dual-input VR experience: hand tracking with pinch-to-select for menu interaction and grab for object manipulation, automatic transition to controller mode when the player picks up controllers, custom thumbs-up gesture for confirming actions, and a hand tracking quality indicator that fades to a warning icon when confidence drops. Testing in varied lighting conditions to demonstrate reliability boundaries.
setup_requirements: Meta Quest headset with hand tracking enabled in settings
Unity XR Hands package installed and configured
Hand mesh or skeleton visualization assets
Test scene with both near-field and far-field interactable objects

review.value: Using different distance thresholds for pinch-start (fingers close together) and pinch-end (fingers farther apart) to prevent rapid on/off flickering when the finger distance is near the threshold. Typical values: start at 1.5cm, end at 3cm.
The angle of flexion of each finger, calculated from the joint chain. Fully extended = 0 degrees, fully curled = ~160 degrees. Used to detect open hand vs fist for grab gestures.

The SDK's estimate of tracking quality for each hand. High confidence means all joints are accurately tracked. Low confidence means the hand is partially occluded or in poor lighting. The application should provide visual feedback when confidence is low.

The Quest automatically detects when controllers are held or set down. The application should smoothly fade between controller models and hand meshes, and adjust interaction behavior (controller ray vs hand pinch) accordingly.

learning_objectives: Implement teleportation, continuous, and room-scale locomotion for VR
Understand the physiological causes of VR motion sickness and apply evidence-based mitigations
Design locomotion systems that offer player choice in comfort settings
Evaluate locomotion tradeoffs between freedom of movement and user comfort
readings: Oculus VR Best Practices Guide - Comfort and Health

lecture_notes.introduction: Moving through virtual space without physically walking creates a sensory conflict between your visual system and vestibular system. Your eyes see motion, but your inner ear detects no corresponding acceleration. This mismatch is the primary cause of VR-induced motion sickness, which can range from mild unease to severe nausea. Every locomotion choice is a tradeoff between freedom of movement and user comfort. Teleportation is the most comfortable method because there is no continuous visual motion that conflicts with the vestibular system -- the player points, the screen blinks, and they are at the new location. But teleportation feels disconnected and breaks immersion. Continuous thumbstick locomotion feels natural and provides full movement freedom, but causes nausea in a significant percentage of users, especially during rotation. Room-scale movement -- physically walking within the tracked play space -- is perfectly comfortable because visual and vestibular inputs match, but it is limited to the physical room size. Today we implement all three locomotion methods, add configurable comfort settings (vignetting intensity, snap turn angle, movement speed), and create a comfort settings menu accessible from within VR. We discuss the physiology of motion sickness, the specific visual patterns that trigger it (optic flow in peripheral vision, rotational motion, altitude changes), and the evidence-based mitigations that reduce it without eliminating movement.
key_concepts: Teleportation: arc pointer visualization, valid/invalid landing indicators, blink or fade transition, no motion sickness
Continuous locomotion: thumbstick-driven, head-directed vs hand-directed movement, causes nausea in susceptible users
Room-scale movement: physical walking within guardian boundaries, 1:1 visual-vestibular match, limited by room size
Snap turning: discrete rotation in 15-45 degree increments, eliminates continuous rotational optic flow that triggers nausea
Smooth turning: continuous rotation, more immersive but triggers nausea more than any other locomotion component
Vignetting (tunneling): darkening peripheral vision during artificial movement, reducing the optic flow mismatch area
Comfort settings menu: player-configurable options for turn type, turn speed, vignette intensity, and movement speed
Guardian/boundary system: visible walls that appear when the player approaches the physical play space boundary
Redirected walking: subtly rotating the virtual world during physical walking to fit a larger virtual space in a smaller room
Vestibular conflict model: nausea occurs when visual motion (optic flow) contradicts vestibular signals (no acceleration detected)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a multi-mode locomotion system: teleportation with an arc pointer that changes color over valid/invalid surfaces and blink-to-target with a brief fade, continuous thumbstick movement with head-directed steering, snap turn and smooth turn toggle, and an in-VR comfort settings panel where the player adjusts vignette intensity (0-100%), snap turn angle (15/30/45/90), and movement speed. Testing each mode and measuring the impact of comfort settings on the experience.
setup_requirements: Unity XR project with XR Interaction Toolkit locomotion providers
Large virtual environment (outdoor or open building) for testing traversal
Quest headset with guardian boundaries configured
Comfort settings UI prefab with slider and toggle controls

review.value: Dynamically reducing peripheral field of view during artificial locomotion by fading screen edges to black. Reduces nausea because peripheral vision is most sensitive to optic flow mismatch. Typical implementations scale vignette intensity with movement speed.

Rotating the viewpoint in discrete increments (typically 30-45 degrees) rather than smoothly. Eliminates continuous rotational optic flow, which is the strongest single trigger for VR nausea. Most users tolerate snap turn well even if they are susceptible to motion sickness.

The screen fades to black over 100-200ms, the player position changes, then the screen fades back in. The blackout prevents any visual motion during position change. Some implementations show a brief preview of the destination before the blink.

Head-directed movement moves in the direction the player is looking. Hand-directed moves in the direction the controller points. Head-directed is simpler but means you cannot look sideways while moving forward. Hand-directed provides more freedom.

class_schedule.topic: VR Comfort and Motion Sickness Mitigation
learning_objectives: Apply a comprehensive framework of comfort techniques to VR experiences
Design content that minimizes motion sickness triggers while maintaining immersion
Implement adaptive comfort systems that respond to detected user discomfort
Conduct structured comfort testing sessions with standardized assessment tools
readings: IEEE VR 2023: Adaptive Comfort in Virtual Reality
The VR Book (Jerald) Ch. 12 - Health and Safety

lecture_notes.introduction: Motion sickness is not a flaw in the user -- it is a flaw in the design. If your VR experience makes users sick, the experience is broken. Approximately 40-60% of VR users experience some degree of motion sickness with continuous locomotion, and even among experienced VR users, prolonged exposure to comfort-violating stimuli causes cumulative discomfort. As VR developers, we have an ethical obligation to design experiences that are accessible to the widest possible audience, including users who are highly susceptible to motion sickness. Today we go beyond basic vignetting and snap turn to build a comprehensive comfort framework. We examine every element of a VR scene that affects comfort: camera control (never move the camera without player initiation), velocity profiles (acceleration and deceleration curves that minimize sensory conflict), stable reference frames (cockpits, vehicle interiors, visible noses), altitude management (vertical movement is more nauseating than horizontal), and rest frames (periodic moments of visual stability). We implement an adaptive comfort system that monitors proxy indicators of discomfort (head movement instability, postural sway estimation) and automatically increases comfort measures when signs of discomfort are detected.
key_concepts: Camera control rules: never move the VR camera without player initiation, never override head tracking, never change FOV unexpectedly
Velocity profiles: gradual acceleration and deceleration reduce sensory conflict compared to instant speed changes
Stable reference frames: cockpit interiors, vehicle dashboards, visible nose models that provide a stationary visual anchor
Altitude comfort: vertical movement (elevators, ramps, falling) is more nauseating than horizontal; use teleportation or cuts for height changes
Rest frames: periodic moments of zero artificial motion (reaching a platform, opening a menu) that let the vestibular system recalibrate
Comfort rating system: Meta's Comfortable/Moderate/Intense rating framework for categorizing VR content
Simulator Sickness Questionnaire (SSQ): standardized assessment tool with nausea, oculomotor, and disorientation subscales
Adaptation period: users become less susceptible to motion sickness with gradual exposure (VR legs), but should never be forced to push through discomfort
Content design for comfort: ground-level gameplay, stable horizons, limited camera-initiated movement, clear sky/ceiling reference
Accessibility spectrum: designing for the most susceptible users first, with optional comfort reductions for experienced users
lecture_notes.demonstration: Auditing an existing VR demo for comfort violations: identifying a camera shake effect on impact (violation: camera movement without player initiation), instant-velocity continuous locomotion (violation: no acceleration curve), vertical elevator ride (violation: sustained altitude change with visual motion), and a cutscene that overrides camera rotation (violation: head tracking override). Fixing each issue and conducting a before/after comfort comparison using the SSQ questionnaire.
setup_requirements: Unity XR project with an existing demo containing comfort issues
Comfort audit checklist document
SSQ questionnaire forms (printed or digital)
Test participants for comfort evaluation

review.value: The single most important comfort rule. If the player's head moves but the virtual camera does not match, extreme nausea results within seconds. Even cutscenes must allow the player to look around freely.

A visually stationary element at a fixed position in the player's view. A cockpit, vehicle interior, or even a subtle nose model provides the visual system with a reference that matches the vestibular system's 'no motion' signal.

The Simulator Sickness Questionnaire produces a total severity score and three subscale scores (nausea, oculomotor, disorientation). Scores above 15 indicate noticeable discomfort. Testing should be conducted with 10+ participants for meaningful results.

Meta's three-tier system: Comfortable (stationary or teleport-only experiences), Moderate (limited smooth locomotion with comfort options), Intense (sustained artificial locomotion, fast movement, altitude changes). All content should be rated and the rating disclosed to users.

learning_objectives: Design and implement UI systems that exist in 3D virtual space
Create diegetic, spatial, and meta UI paradigms appropriate for different contexts
Handle text rendering, readability, and input in VR environments
Build accessible VR menus that work with both controllers and hand tracking
readings: 3D User Interfaces: Theory and Practice Ch. 7-9
Unity XR UI Input Module Documentation
Apple Human Interface Guidelines for visionOS - Spatial UI

lecture_notes.introduction: Traditional 2D UI breaks down in VR. There is no screen to overlay UI onto. Menus cannot hover in screen space because there is no screen -- the player's entire visual field is the 3D environment. Text must be readable at varying distances and angles. Buttons must be targetable with imprecise 3D pointing or hand tracking, not pixel-precise mouse clicks. VR UI design has converged on three paradigms: diegetic UI (interfaces embedded in the game world, like a wrist-mounted health display or a physical clipboard), spatial UI (world-space panels floating in the environment, like a settings menu anchored to the player's left hand), and meta UI (elements that exist in the player's peripheral vision and follow head movement, like a subtle compass or notification). Each paradigm has different strengths: diegetic UI maintains immersion, spatial UI is familiar and flexible, and meta UI is always available without cluttering the environment. Text rendering in VR requires special attention because headset resolution limits readability. SDF (Signed Distance Field) fonts provide sharp text at any distance, but minimum font size depends on the headset's pixels-per-degree and the panel's distance from the player. Today we implement all three UI paradigms, build a complete settings menu navigable by both controller ray and hand pinch, and solve the text readability challenge.
key_concepts: Diegetic UI: interfaces embedded in the game world as physical objects (wrist watch, clipboard, in-world screens)
Spatial UI: world-space panels positioned in 3D space, often anchored to the player's hand or a fixed world position
Meta UI: heads-up display elements that follow head rotation with lag, existing in peripheral vision (compass, alerts)
World-space Canvas: Unity UI Canvas set to World Space render mode, positioned and scaled for VR readability
SDF text rendering: TextMeshPro with Signed Distance Field fonts for sharp text at any distance and angle in VR
Minimum readable text size: 1 degree of visual angle minimum, approximately 1.5mm per meter of distance from the player
Curved UI panels: bending UI canvases to follow a cylindrical or spherical surface for comfortable reading across wide panels
XR UI Input Module: Unity's input module that routes XR ray or poke input to standard UI event system (buttons, sliders, scrollviews)
Gaze-and-dwell: alternative selection method where looking at a target for a duration (1-2 seconds) selects it, useful for accessibility
Keyboard input in VR: virtual keyboards rendered in 3D space, typed with finger poke or controller ray, with predictive text
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a complete VR menu system: a main menu as a spatial panel that appears attached to the left controller when a button is pressed, with settings for locomotion, comfort, audio, and graphics. The menu uses curved UI for comfortable reading, SDF text at readable sizes, and accepts input from both controller ray (trigger to select) and hand pinch. A diegetic wrist menu shows health and inventory, revealed by turning the wrist to look at it. Text sizes are validated at 1m and 2m distances.
setup_requirements: Unity XR project with XR Interaction Toolkit and XR UI Input Module
TextMeshPro with SDF font assets configured
Canvas prefabs for spatial and diegetic UI panels
Hand tracking wrist detection implementation

review.value: Text must subtend at least 1 degree of visual angle to be readable. At 1 meter distance, this means ~1.7cm font height minimum. SDF fonts remain sharp regardless of distance; bitmap fonts become blurry. Always test readability on-headset.

A UI panel attached to the player's wrist that appears when the player looks at their wrist (detected by wrist-to-head angle). Maintains immersion because it exists as a physical object in the game world. Used for frequently accessed information like health, ammo, or inventory.

Bending a flat UI panel into a cylindrical surface centered on the player. Ensures all parts of a wide panel are equidistant from the player's eyes, maintaining consistent text sharpness and reducing the need for eye convergence changes.

Bridges XR input (ray interactors, poke interactors) to Unity's standard Event System. Allows existing UI Buttons, Sliders, and ScrollViews to work with VR controllers and hand tracking without rewriting UI code.

learning_objectives: Implement HRTF-based spatial audio for accurate 3D sound positioning
Design audio environments that enhance presence and provide navigational cues
Configure distance attenuation, occlusion, and room acoustics for VR spaces
Integrate spatial audio SDKs for hardware-accelerated binaural rendering
readings: Game Audio with FMOD Ch. 10-12 - Spatial Audio
AES Convention Paper: Binaural Audio for VR (overview)

lecture_notes.introduction: In VR, audio is not stereo -- it exists in 3D space just like visual elements. Your ears are as important as your eyes for spatial presence. When a character speaks from your left, you should hear their voice from the left. When they walk behind you, the sound should move behind your head. When they enter another room, the acoustics should change. This requires spatial audio: rendering sound as if it comes from specific 3D positions in the virtual world, using Head-Related Transfer Functions (HRTFs) that model how sound is filtered by the shape of your ears, head, and torso. An HRTF transforms a mono sound source into a binaural (two-ear) signal that your brain interprets as coming from a specific direction and distance. Beyond directionality, VR audio needs distance attenuation (sounds get quieter with distance following an inverse-square law), occlusion (sounds are muffled when a wall is between source and listener), room acoustics (reverb characteristics that match the visual space), and Doppler effect (pitch shift from moving sources). Today we integrate Meta's Spatial Audio SDK and configure a scene with multiple spatially-positioned audio sources, occluding geometry, and room-specific reverb zones. We also discuss the importance of audio-visual congruence: when what you hear matches what you see in position and timing, presence is enhanced; when they mismatch, immersion breaks.
key_concepts: HRTF: Head-Related Transfer Function modeling how ears, head, and torso filter sound from different directions
Binaural audio: two-channel audio rendered through headphones that creates the perception of 3D sound positioning
Distance attenuation: volume decrease with distance, typically inverse-square law with a minimum audible distance parameter
Sound occlusion: filtering and attenuating sound when geometry blocks the direct path between source and listener
Room acoustics simulation: reverb characteristics (early reflections, late reverb, RT60 decay time) matched to visual room size and materials
Ambisonic soundfields: spherical harmonic encoding of environmental audio that rotates with head tracking for immersive ambiences
Audio-visual congruence: matching audio spatial position and timing with visual sources to maintain presence
Spatialization falloff curves: configuring how spatialization transitions from fully spatialized (nearby) to stereo mix (distant)
Audio propagation: simulating how sound travels through open doorways and around corners using portal-based routing
Performance budgeting for audio: spatial audio processing cost scales with source count; limiting simultaneous spatialized sources to 20-30
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building an immersive audio environment in a VR scene: placing spatial audio sources for a ticking clock, a fireplace, and ambient music player at their visual locations. Configuring occlusion so the clock sound is muffled when walking into the next room and re-emerges clearly through the open doorway. Setting up room-specific reverb zones for a small bathroom (short reverb) and a large hall (long reverb). Adding ambisonic environmental audio that rotates correctly with head tracking.
setup_requirements: Unity XR project with Meta Spatial Audio SDK installed
Multi-room VR environment with doors and walls
Audio source files: ambient sounds, spatial effects, room tones
Acoustic material assignments for walls, floors, and furnishings

review.value: A function describing how sound is filtered by ears, head, and torso before reaching the eardrums. Applying the correct HRTF to a mono source creates binaural audio that the brain perceives as coming from a specific 3D position. Generic HRTFs work for most people; personalized HRTFs improve accuracy.

When geometry blocks the direct path between a sound source and the listener, the sound is filtered (reducing high frequencies) and attenuated (reducing volume). Implemented by raycasting from source to listener and applying a low-pass filter when the ray hits geometry.

The time for a sound to decay by 60dB in a room. A small bathroom has RT60 of ~0.3s (short, tight reverb). A cathedral has RT60 of 3-5s (long, spacious reverb). Matching reverb to visual room size is critical for VR presence.

A spherical harmonic encoding of a sound field that captures audio from all directions. When decoded for headphones with head tracking, the sound field rotates naturally as the listener turns their head, creating immersive environmental audio.

learning_objectives: Implement AR marker detection and image tracking using AR Foundation
Build phone-based AR experiences with ARKit and ARCore through Unity
Track multiple image markers simultaneously and anchor virtual content to them
Handle marker tracking quality, distance limits, and environmental requirements
readings: ARKit Documentation - Image Detection and Tracking
Unity AR Foundation Documentation - Image Tracking

lecture_notes.introduction: Augmented reality overlays virtual content onto the real world, and marker tracking is the most reliable method for precisely placing virtual objects at specific real-world locations. A marker is a known image -- a poster, a product label, a business card -- that the AR system can recognize and track in the camera feed. When the system detects a marker, it determines the marker's position and orientation in 3D space relative to the camera, enabling virtual content to be anchored to that physical location. AR Foundation is Unity's cross-platform AR framework that provides a unified API for ARKit (iOS) and ARCore (Android), abstracting the platform-specific implementations behind common interfaces for image tracking, plane detection, raycasting, and more. Today we build an AR image tracking application: creating a reference image library, configuring tracking for multiple markers, spawning 3D content at each detected marker's position, and handling tracking state changes (tracking, limited, none) gracefully. We also discuss the practical limitations of marker tracking: markers must be flat, well-lit, and have sufficient visual complexity; tracking degrades at distance (typically beyond 1.5-2m for small markers); and fast camera movement causes tracking loss.
key_concepts: AR Foundation architecture: AR Session, AR Session Origin, platform-specific providers for ARKit and ARCore
Reference image library: a database of known images with their physical dimensions, compiled into a trackable asset
Image tracking lifecycle: Added (first detected), Updated (tracked, position refined), Removed (lost from view)
Tracking state: Full (confident), Limited (low confidence, intermittent), None (lost entirely), and visual feedback for each
Marker requirements: high contrast, asymmetric pattern, minimum 15cm for reliable tracking, flat and non-reflective surface
Multiple simultaneous markers: tracking several images at once, each spawning independent virtual content
Content anchoring: attaching virtual GameObjects to tracked images so they move with the marker in the real world
Scale accuracy: providing the correct physical marker size ensures virtual content is at the correct real-world scale
Lighting estimation: AR Foundation provides estimated environmental lighting for realistic virtual object shading
Camera permission handling: requesting and responding to camera access permission on both iOS and Android
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a phone-based AR app that tracks three different printed images: a product poster spawns a 3D animated character, a QR-code-style marker spawns an info panel, and a physical game board spawns interactive game pieces. Each marker's content appears, tracks smoothly as the phone moves, and disappears with a fade when the marker is lost. Lighting estimation adjusts the virtual objects' shading to match the room.
setup_requirements: Unity project with AR Foundation and ARKit/ARCore XR Plugins
Three printed reference images of different types
iOS or Android device with AR capability
3D models and UI prefabs for spawned AR content

review.value: A compiled database of images the AR system can recognize. Each entry includes the source image and its physical width. The library is processed at build time to extract feature points for efficient real-time matching.

Markers transition between Added (first seen), Updated (actively tracked with position updates), and Removed (lost). Content should fade in on Added, stay visible during Updated, and fade out on Removed rather than appearing/disappearing instantly.
The AR system uses the known physical width of the marker to calculate the distance and orientation. If the physical size is wrong, virtual content will be at the wrong scale and distance.

AR Foundation estimates the ambient light intensity and color temperature from the camera feed. Applying this to virtual objects' lights and materials makes them blend convincingly with the real environment.

learning_objectives: Implement plane detection for placing virtual content on real-world surfaces
Create spatial anchors that persist virtual content across sessions
Build mixed reality experiences that interact with detected room geometry
Handle the transition from AR plane detection to VR passthrough mixed reality
readings: Meta Quest Mixed Reality Developer Guide
Unity AR Foundation - Plane Manager Documentation

lecture_notes.introduction: While marker tracking anchors content to specific known images, plane detection allows content to be placed on any flat surface in the real world: tables, floors, walls, and ceilings. Combined with spatial anchors that persist content positions across sessions, plane detection enables the full spectrum of augmented reality experiences. The AR system analyzes the camera feed and depth sensors to identify flat surfaces, estimating their position, orientation, and boundary polygon. Your application can then raycast against these detected planes to place content where the user taps. The Quest 3 extends this concept further with its Scene API, which provides a full 3D understanding of the room: walls, floor, ceiling, furniture, and door/window openings. This enables mixed reality experiences where virtual content not only sits on real surfaces but is occluded by real furniture and interacts with the physical room geometry. Today we implement plane detection for phone-based AR content placement, spatial anchors for persistent positioning, and Quest 3 mixed reality with room-scale scene understanding. We also address the UX challenges of AR placement: guiding the user to scan their environment, providing visual feedback during surface detection, and handling the common case where the detected plane extends beyond the physical surface boundary.
key_concepts: Plane detection: identifying horizontal (floors, tables) and vertical (walls) flat surfaces in the camera feed
Plane boundary: the estimated polygon edge of a detected plane, used for visualizing and constraining content placement
AR raycast: casting a ray from a screen touch point into the AR scene to find the intersection with a detected plane
Spatial anchors: persistent references to physical locations that survive app restarts and can be shared between devices
Cloud anchors: spatial anchors uploaded to a cloud service (Google Cloud Anchors, Azure Spatial Anchors) for multi-user AR
Scene mesh: real-time 3D reconstruction of the physical environment using depth sensors, available on Quest 3 and LiDAR devices
Occlusion: rendering virtual objects behind real-world surfaces by writing scene mesh depth to the depth buffer
Passthrough mixed reality: Quest 3's camera-fed view of the real world with virtual content composited on top
Scene API: Meta's system providing semantic understanding of room elements (wall, floor, ceiling, table, couch, door, window)
Content placement UX: scanning animation, surface indicator, tap-to-place, confirm-and-lock workflow for intuitive AR placement
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building two AR placement experiences: (1) Phone-based AR using AR Foundation plane detection to place a virtual pet on a detected table surface, with a tap-to-place raycast and spatial anchor to remember the position across app restarts. (2) Quest 3 mixed reality using Scene API to detect the room layout, place virtual decorations on real walls, and enable the virtual pet to walk on the real floor with occlusion from real furniture.
setup_requirements: Unity project with AR Foundation configured for plane detection
Meta Quest 3 with Scene API room setup completed
iOS or Android device with AR capability for phone-based demo
Virtual pet character with walk animation for placement demo

review.value: A ray from the camera through a screen point that checks for intersections with AR-tracked geometry (planes, meshes, point clouds). The result provides a world-space pose (position + rotation) for placing content aligned to the detected surface.

Persistent coordinate references tied to physical locations using environmental features. The anchor stores the relationship to tracked visual features so virtual content stays in the correct physical position across sessions.

Rendering the real-time 3D mesh of the physical environment to the depth buffer (but not the color buffer) so virtual objects are hidden when behind real furniture or walls. Creates the illusion that virtual content exists within the physical space.

Meta's room understanding system that identifies and labels room elements: walls, floor, ceiling, tables, couches, and door/window openings. Provides anchor-aligned bounding boxes for each element, enabling semantic interaction with the real environment.

learning_objectives: Design experiences that blend virtual and physical environments on the reality-virtuality continuum
Implement passthrough-based mixed reality with virtual content composited on the real world
Create interactions that bridge physical and virtual objects
Evaluate the design space between fully virtual VR and fully real AR
readings: Mixed Reality: Merging Real and Virtual Worlds (Ohta & Tamura) Ch. 1-3
Apple visionOS Human Interface Guidelines - Immersion Levels

lecture_notes.introduction: Mixed reality occupies the space between fully virtual VR and fully real AR on the reality-virtuality continuum. Rather than choosing between complete immersion in a virtual world or thin digital overlays on the real world, mixed reality blends both: virtual objects sit on your real desk, virtual characters hide behind your real couch, and virtual portals open in your real walls to reveal fantasy worlds beyond. The Quest 3 and Apple Vision Pro represent two different approaches to mixed reality. Quest 3 uses high-resolution color passthrough cameras to show a video feed of the real world, onto which virtual content is composited. Vision Pro uses a similar approach with higher-fidelity passthrough and eye tracking for natural focus. Both enable experiences where users can see their physical environment while interacting with virtual content that appears to coexist with it. The design space is vast: a mixed reality game could have creatures that hide under your real furniture, a productivity app that places virtual monitors on your real desk, or an educational experience that dissects a virtual frog on your real table. Today we explore the design principles of mixed reality: when to show the real world vs occlude it, how to handle the boundary between real and virtual spaces, how to make virtual objects feel like they belong in the physical environment, and how to design interactions that bridge the physical-virtual divide.
key_concepts: Reality-virtuality continuum: the spectrum from fully real to fully virtual, with AR and MR as intermediate points
Passthrough rendering: using camera feeds to show the real environment inside the headset, enabling virtual overlay
Immersion levels: full VR (no real world), mixed reality (real world visible with virtual additions), AR (mostly real with minimal virtual)
Virtual-physical interaction: virtual objects bouncing off real surfaces, casting virtual shadows on real floors
Portal effects: openings in the real world that reveal virtual environments, creating dramatic transitions between realities
Boundary management: defining where the real world ends and virtual content begins, handling edge transitions
Environmental coherence: matching virtual lighting, shadows, and reflections to the real environment for believable compositing
User presence awareness: detecting when other people enter the tracked space and adjusting content to avoid conflicts
Shared mixed reality: multiple users seeing the same virtual content in the same physical space using cloud anchors
Design patterns for MR: world-locked content (stays in place), body-locked content (moves with user), and space-adaptive content (adjusts to room geometry)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a mixed reality experience on Quest 3: a virtual aquarium that uses the real wall as its back surface (portal effect), with fish that swim out through the portal into the real room, navigate around real furniture using scene mesh collision, and return to the aquarium. Virtual water effects spill from the portal onto the real floor. The experience adjusts its content placement based on the room's Scene API data to fit any physical space.
setup_requirements: Meta Quest 3 with passthrough enabled and room scanned
Unity XR project with Meta Mixed Reality SDK
Fish character models with swim animations
Portal shader that renders passthrough inside the portal frame

review.value: The headset renders the camera feed of the real world as the background, then renders virtual content on top. Proper compositing requires matching the virtual camera FOV to the passthrough FOV and handling depth so virtual objects can appear behind real objects.

A virtual frame placed on a real surface (wall, floor) that renders a different environment behind it, visible only through the frame. Uses stencil buffer or render texture to mask the virtual world to the portal shape.

Virtual content that adjusts its layout and scale based on the detected room geometry. A game designed for a 3mx3m room scales its play area to fit a 2mx4m room. Content avoids detected furniture and uses walls and floor as interaction surfaces.

Matching virtual content's lighting to the real environment so it looks like it belongs. Use lighting estimation for intensity and color temperature, cast virtual shadows onto real surfaces, and match reflection intensity to ambient brightness.

learning_objectives: Optimize VR/AR applications to meet strict frame rate requirements for immersive comfort
Apply foveated rendering and stereo optimization techniques specific to XR
Implement Application SpaceWarp as a safety net for occasional frame drops
Profile XR applications using Meta-specific and Unity profiling tools

lecture_notes.introduction: VR has the strictest performance requirements of any real-time application. At 90Hz on Quest 3, you have 11.1 milliseconds per frame to render the scene twice (once per eye), run physics, execute game logic, and submit to the compositor. Miss that deadline and the compositor will reproject the previous frame, causing visible artifacts. Miss it repeatedly and the user gets sick. There is no 'mostly 90fps' in VR -- you either hit the target consistently or you do not. The optimization techniques for VR build on standard 3D optimization but add XR-specific methods. Single-pass stereo rendering draws both eyes in one pass instead of two, nearly eliminating the CPU overhead of duplicate draw calls. Fixed foveated rendering reduces the resolution in the periphery of each eye, exploiting the fact that human peripheral vision has much lower acuity than central vision. Application SpaceWarp generates intermediate frames using motion vectors when the application cannot maintain the full frame rate, effectively allowing 45fps rendering at 90Hz display. Today we take a VR scene from an unacceptable 45fps to a stable 90fps using a systematic optimization approach: profiling to identify the bottleneck (CPU or GPU), applying the highest-impact VR-specific optimizations first, and measuring the improvement after each change.
key_concepts: VR frame budgets: 13.9ms at 72Hz, 11.1ms at 90Hz, 8.3ms at 120Hz, including both eyes and compositor submission
Single-pass stereo rendering: drawing both eye views in a single render pass using instanced draw calls with per-eye view matrices
Fixed foveated rendering (FFR): rendering the center of each eye at full resolution with reduced peripheral resolution in 2-3 concentric zones
Application SpaceWarp (ASW): runtime-generated intermediate frames using color, depth, and motion vectors when the app renders at half rate
Multiview rendering: OpenGL ES extension that renders both eyes simultaneously in a single draw call per object
VR-specific profiling: OVR Metrics Tool for real-time overlay, RenderDoc for GPU frame capture, Unity Profiler for CPU analysis
GPU-bound vs CPU-bound diagnosis: comparing CPU frame time, GPU frame time, and compositor wait to identify the bottleneck
Draw call budgets for VR: 150-200 draw calls maximum on Quest standalone, with batching and instancing to consolidate
Texture memory on standalone VR: Quest 3 has shared 8GB RAM; textures should use ASTC compression aggressively
Shader complexity for mobile VR: avoid expensive fragment operations, minimize texture samples, prefer half-precision math
lecture_notes.demonstration: Taking a VR scene from 45fps to 90fps: starting with profiling (OVR Metrics Tool shows GPU-bound at 22ms/frame with 350 draw calls). Step 1: Enable single-pass stereo rendering (saves 4ms by eliminating duplicate draw calls). Step 2: Enable fixed foveated rendering at medium level (saves 3ms by reducing peripheral fragment shading). Step 3: Reduce draw calls from 350 to 120 using static batching and GPU instancing (saves 2ms). Step 4: Compress textures from RGBA to ASTC 6x6 (reduces GPU memory bandwidth, saves 1ms). Result: 12ms GPU frame time, comfortably within 11.1ms budget after headroom.
setup_requirements: Meta Quest headset with OVR Metrics Tool installed
Unity XR project with a performance-heavy VR scene
Unity Profiler configured for XR remote profiling over USB
Oculus Developer Hub for device monitoring and sideloading

review.value: Rendering the center of each eye at full resolution while reducing the outer zones to 1/2 or 1/4 resolution. Saves 20-40% fragment shading cost. The quality reduction is barely visible because human peripheral vision has low acuity.

When the app cannot maintain 90fps, ASW lets it render at 45fps while the runtime generates intermediate frames by reprojecting using color, depth, and motion vectors. Artifacts appear on fast-moving objects and disocclusion edges but are acceptable as a fallback.

Instead of rendering the scene twice (once per eye) with separate draw calls, single-pass stereo uses GPU instancing to render both eye views in a single pass. Eliminates nearly all the CPU overhead of the second eye and reduces GPU vertex processing.

If GPU frame time exceeds CPU frame time and the compositor is not waiting, the scene is GPU-bound. Solutions target GPU workload: foveated rendering, fewer draw calls, simpler shaders, lower resolution, fewer lights, reduced overdraw.

learning_objectives: Design XR experiences that are accessible to users with diverse abilities
Implement seated, standing, and room-scale play modes for different physical capabilities
Address visual, auditory, motor, and cognitive accessibility in VR/AR applications
Apply inclusive design principles that improve the experience for all users
readings: XR Accessibility User Requirements (W3C Working Draft)

lecture_notes.introduction: XR technology has the potential to be profoundly inclusive or profoundly exclusive. A well-designed VR experience can give a wheelchair user the ability to explore environments they cannot access physically. A poorly designed one can exclude anyone who cannot stand for 30 minutes, raise their arms above their head, or see fine detail in low-resolution headset displays. Accessibility in XR is not an afterthought -- it is a design requirement that, when done well, improves the experience for everyone. Seated play mode with adjustable height benefits not only wheelchair users but also people on long flights, in office settings, or simply preferring comfort. Subtitle systems for VR audio help not only deaf users but also people playing in noisy environments. Adjustable text sizes help not only visually impaired users but everyone when headset resolution is limited. Today we implement a comprehensive accessibility system for a VR application: seated and standing modes with adjustable floor offset, one-handed interaction alternatives for motor-impaired users, visual indicators for audio cues (subtitle system, visual sound direction indicators), colorblind-safe UI palettes, adjustable text size and contrast, comfort options as accessibility features, and gaze-and-dwell as an alternative selection method for users who cannot reliably perform pinch or trigger actions.
key_concepts: Seated play mode: adjustable virtual floor height, reachable interactions within seated arm range, no required standing or walking
One-handed interaction: all core functions accessible with a single controller, remappable controls, auto-grab toggle option
Visual accessibility: adjustable text size and contrast, colorblind-safe color palettes, high-contrast mode for UI elements
Audio accessibility: spatial subtitle system showing who is speaking and from what direction, visual sound direction indicators
Motor accessibility: adjustable interaction distances, sticky grab (toggle instead of hold), reduced precision requirements
Cognitive accessibility: simplified UI modes, tutorial replay options, adjustable game speed, clear and consistent interaction patterns
Comfort as accessibility: motion sickness sensitivity varies widely; robust comfort options are accessibility features
Gaze-and-dwell selection: looking at a target for a configurable duration to select it, alternative to physical button press
Recenter and reorient: allowing the player to reset their forward direction and seated position at any time
Inclusive design mindset: designing for the full spectrum of human ability from the start rather than retrofitting accessibility
lecture_notes.demonstration: Adding accessibility features to an existing VR experience: implementing a seated mode toggle that adjusts all interaction heights and locomotion to work from a chair, a one-handed mode that remaps two-controller interactions to a single controller, a subtitle system that displays spatial audio as text with directional indicators, a gaze-and-dwell fallback for the selection interaction, and a high-contrast UI toggle. Testing each feature and demonstrating that the core experience is fully playable with each accessibility option enabled.
setup_requirements: Unity XR project with a playable VR experience from previous weeks
Subtitle system UI prefab with directional indicators
Accessibility settings menu with toggle and slider options
Test scenario that requires locomotion, interaction, and UI navigation

review.value: Adjusts the virtual floor height so the player's seated eye height maps to standing eye height in the virtual world. All interactable objects and UI panels are repositioned to be within seated arm's reach. Locomotion works normally.

The player looks at a target, a radial timer fills over 1-2 seconds, and the selection activates. Useful for users who cannot reliably press buttons. Duration should be configurable to balance speed for able users and reliability for motor-impaired users.

Text displayed in VR that shows dialogue with a directional indicator (arrow or speaker name) pointing toward the sound source. Positioned in the player's comfortable reading zone, not world-locked (which would require head turning to read).

Accessibility features often improve the experience for all users. Subtitles help in noisy environments. Seated mode enables playing on an airplane. One-handed mode is useful when the other hand is holding a drink. Comfort options serve VR-newcomers.

learning_objectives: Design and conduct structured user testing sessions for VR/AR applications
Measure presence, usability, and comfort using validated assessment instruments
Observe and interpret user behavior in XR without disrupting the immersive experience
Iterate on design based on user testing feedback with evidence-based prioritization
readings: Games User Research Ch. 15 - VR User Testing

lecture_notes.introduction: User testing XR experiences presents unique challenges that do not exist in traditional game testing. The tester is wearing a headset that isolates them from the outside world, so you cannot see their screen. Traditional screen recording does not capture the spatial experience. The tester may experience physical discomfort (motion sickness, eye strain, neck fatigue) that is not present in flatscreen testing. And the researcher cannot easily communicate with the tester without breaking immersion. Despite these challenges, user testing is even more important in XR than in traditional games because the consequences of poor design are more severe -- discomfort, nausea, and disorientation rather than just frustration. Today we design and conduct a complete XR user testing protocol: pre-test screening (motion sickness susceptibility, VR experience level, physical limitations), task-based testing with think-aloud protocol adapted for VR (speaking aloud while immersed), observation using spectator mode or mirror screen, post-test assessment using the Simulator Sickness Questionnaire (SSQ) for comfort and the System Usability Scale (SUS) for usability, and a semi-structured interview for qualitative insights. We discuss how to interpret the data, prioritize findings, and iterate on design.
key_concepts: Pre-test screening: motion sickness susceptibility (MSSQ), VR experience level, physical limitations, IPD measurement
XR think-aloud protocol: asking participants to verbalize thoughts while immersed, with neutral prompts if silent for 30+ seconds
Spectator mode observation: displaying the VR view on an external monitor for the researcher to observe without headset access
Mixed reality capture: recording the participant's physical movements alongside their VR view for holistic analysis
Simulator Sickness Questionnaire (SSQ): 16 symptoms rated on a 4-point scale, producing nausea, oculomotor, and disorientation subscores
System Usability Scale (SUS): 10-item questionnaire producing a 0-100 usability score, applicable to XR with minor wording adaptation
Presence questionnaire: measuring the degree to which the user felt 'present' in the virtual environment (involvement, sensory fidelity)
Task completion metrics: success rate, time on task, error count, and help requests for each defined test task
Physical comfort observation: noting when participants adjust the headset, rub their eyes, shift their weight, or grab furniture for balance
Iterative testing: test early with paper prototypes and storyboards, test mid-development with greybox builds, test late with polished builds
lecture_notes.demonstration: Conducting a live XR user testing session: administering a pre-test screening questionnaire, briefing the participant (emphasizing we are testing the application, not them), starting the VR experience with spectator mode for observation, taking timestamped notes on interactions, confusion points, and physical comfort indicators, pausing for mid-session SSQ if the session exceeds 15 minutes, completing the full SSQ and SUS post-test, and conducting a 5-minute semi-structured interview. Then analyzing the collected data to produce three prioritized design recommendations.
setup_requirements: VR application build at a testable milestone
Quest headset with spectator mode configured (Oculus casting or scrcpy)
Printed SSQ, SUS, and screening questionnaires
Observation note template with timestamp column
Video recording setup (optional but recommended)

review.value: Administer SSQ before testing (baseline) and after testing (post-exposure). The difference reveals symptoms caused by the experience specifically. A total severity score increase above 15 points indicates meaningful discomfort requiring design intervention.

SUS produces a score from 0-100. Above 68 is above average usability. Above 80 is good. Above 90 is excellent. Below 50 indicates serious usability problems. VR applications typically score lower than desktop apps due to novel interaction paradigms.

VR testing partially mitigates observer effect because the participant cannot see the researcher. However, speaking aloud (think-aloud protocol) feels unnatural while immersed. Give participants 2-3 minutes to acclimate before beginning task testing.

Severity x Frequency matrix: a comfort issue affecting 80% of users is critical, a usability issue affecting 20% is moderate. Comfort issues always take priority over feature issues because discomfort prevents the user from experiencing features at all.

learning_objectives: Present a complete XR experience demonstrating mastery of VR/AR technologies
Articulate design decisions for interaction, comfort, and accessibility with supporting user test data
Demonstrate technical proficiency in XR rendering, interaction, and platform deployment
Reflect on the development process with honest post-mortem analysis
readings: Peer Review Best Practices for XR Presentations

lecture_notes.introduction: Today each student presents their final XR experience: a complete application that integrates VR interaction, AR/MR elements, spatial audio, accessibility features, and performance optimization. This is the capstone of the course and your opportunity to demonstrate mastery of the full XR development pipeline. Your presentation should include a live headset demonstration (with spectator mode for the audience), a walkthrough of your technical architecture and key implementation decisions, user testing results with SSQ and SUS data from at least 3 participants, performance profiling evidence showing you meet frame budget on target hardware, and accessibility features you implemented and why. The written post-mortem reflects on the full development journey: what design decisions worked well, what you would change with the benefit of hindsight, what technical challenges were hardest to solve, and what you learned about designing for spatial computing that you could not have learned from a textbook. XR development is still a frontier field -- the design patterns are not yet settled, the hardware is evolving rapidly, and the best practices are being discovered by practitioners like you. Your experience building and testing a complete XR application is genuinely valuable and positions you for a career in one of the most exciting areas of interactive technology.
key_concepts: Live headset demonstration: spectator mode or casting for the audience, backup recorded video for technical issues
Technical architecture presentation: XR interaction system, locomotion implementation, spatial audio configuration, AR tracking setup
User testing results: SSQ comfort scores, SUS usability scores, task completion rates, and key qualitative findings
Performance evidence: OVR Metrics Tool screenshots showing frame time at or below target, GPU/CPU utilization breakdown
Accessibility demonstration: showing the experience in seated mode, one-handed mode, or with comfort options maximized
Post-mortem analysis: structured reflection on design decisions, technical challenges, user testing surprises, and lessons learned
Peer evaluation: constructive feedback on immersion quality, comfort, interaction design, and technical execution
Portfolio preparation: framing the XR project for job applications in spatial computing, VR/AR studios, and enterprise XR
Future development: features that would improve the experience with more time and resources
Industry context: positioning the project relative to current XR market trends and opportunities
lecture_notes.demonstration: Each student presents for 10-12 minutes with 3-5 minutes Q&A. The presentation includes a live demo on headset with spectator casting, technical architecture walkthrough, user testing data summary, and post-mortem highlights. Audience members try the experience after the presentation for hands-on evaluation.
setup_requirements: Quest headset with final build installed and spectator casting configured
Presentation laptop connected to projector for slides and spectator view
Backup recorded video of the complete experience
Written post-mortem and user testing report submitted before presentation

review.value: Casting the VR headset view to an external screen so the audience can see what the presenter sees. Essential for VR presentations because the audience cannot otherwise evaluate the experience. Use Oculus casting to a Chromecast or scrcpy over USB for minimal latency.

Quantitative data (SSQ scores, SUS scores, task completion rates) from at least 3 test participants, plus qualitative findings from observation and interviews. Evidence should show the final version incorporates changes made in response to testing findings.

The most valuable post-mortems acknowledge failures: a comfort approach that did not work, an interaction that confused every tester, a performance optimization that was harder than expected. Honest reflection demonstrates deeper learning than claiming everything went perfectly.

An XR project demonstrating interaction design, comfort engineering, spatial audio, accessibility, and performance optimization is highly marketable. Include a video walkthrough, technical documentation, and user testing summary in your portfolio.

quizzes.topic: VR Hardware, Spatial Interaction, and Hand Tracking

questions.question: A VR developer is building a game that requires the player to explore large outdoor environments. They choose continuous thumbstick locomotion for movement. During playtesting, 60% of testers report moderate nausea within 15 minutes. Describe three specific comfort techniques the developer should implement, explain the physiological reason each one reduces nausea, and discuss the gameplay tradeoff each introduces.
questions.answer: Three comfort techniques: (1) Dynamic vignetting - darken the peripheral field of view during movement. Physiological reason: peripheral vision is most sensitive to optic flow mismatch with the vestibular system; reducing peripheral visual information reduces the conflict signal. Tradeoff: reduces visual immersion and the player's sense of speed. (2) Snap turning - replace smooth rotation with discrete angular jumps of 30-45 degrees. Physiological reason: continuous rotational optic flow is the strongest nausea trigger because the semicircular canals detect no corresponding rotation. Tradeoff: feels less natural and can be disorienting during fast-paced gameplay. (3) Add a stable visual reference frame such as a visible cockpit or fixed horizon indicator. Physiological reason: provides the visual system with a stationary reference that matches the vestibular report of no motion, reducing the mismatch signal. Tradeoff: restricts art direction and may not fit open-world game settings.

rubric.key: Three distinct comfort techniques described
Correct physiological explanation for each technique
Gameplay tradeoff identified for each

questions.question: Compare pinch gesture and controller trigger press as selection mechanisms in VR. For each, describe one advantage and one disadvantage, and identify which is more appropriate for a precision-demanding task like threading a needle.
questions.answer: Pinch gesture: Advantage - natural and intuitive, no device to hold, leaves hands free when not interacting. Disadvantage - no haptic feedback on selection (the player feels nothing when the pinch threshold is crossed), 20-30ms additional tracking latency compared to controllers, and tracking can degrade when hands overlap or are in poor lighting. Controller trigger: Advantage - tactile feedback confirms selection (the player feels the trigger click), lower latency tracking, and consistent reliability regardless of lighting. Disadvantage - requires holding a physical device, occupies the hand even when not needed, and limits finger articulation to binary inputs. For precision needle-threading: controller trigger is more appropriate because the task requires precise, stable positioning (lower tracking jitter), clear confirmation of when the action is committed (haptic click), and sustained steady hand position (controller tracking is more stable than hand tracking for small, slow movements).

rubric.key: Correct advantage and disadvantage for each input method
Correct choice for precision task with reasoning

questions.question: Explain what interaction affordances are in VR and why they are more important than in traditional screen-based games. Give two specific examples.
questions.answer: Interaction affordances are visual cues that communicate how objects can be manipulated. They are more important in VR than screen-based games because VR lacks a cursor or hover state -- there is no universal pointing device that changes appearance over interactive objects. In a screen game, the mouse cursor changes to a hand icon over clickable objects. In VR, the player's hands are always hands regardless of context, and there is no automatic indication of which objects respond to interaction. Example 1: Proximity glow -- objects emit a subtle glow or outline when the player's hand or controller enters their interaction range, communicating 'you can grab this.' Example 2: Socket highlight -- when the player holds a grabbable object near a compatible socket (inventory slot, puzzle piece receptacle), the socket glows and shows an alignment ghost, communicating 'place this here.'

rubric.key: Correctly defines affordances and explains VR-specific importance
Two specific, distinct VR affordance examples

quizzes.topic: Locomotion, Comfort, and VR UI Design

questions.question: Compare teleportation, continuous locomotion, and room-scale movement in VR. For each, state the comfort level, the freedom of movement, and one game genre where it is the best fit.
questions.answer: Teleportation: Comfort level - high (no continuous visual motion, minimal nausea risk). Freedom of movement - moderate (can reach any visible point but movement is discontinuous). Best genre - puzzle/exploration games (e.g., Myst-like adventures) where continuous movement is unnecessary and immersion is maintained through environmental discovery. Continuous locomotion: Comfort level - low to moderate (causes nausea in 40-60% of users without mitigations, manageable with vignetting and snap turn). Freedom of movement - high (full analog control in any direction at any speed). Best genre - first-person shooter (e.g., Pavlov VR) where tactical movement, strafing, and quick repositioning are core to gameplay. Room-scale movement: Comfort level - very high (perfect visual-vestibular match, no artificial motion). Freedom of movement - low (limited to the physical play space boundary, typically 2m x 2m). Best genre - beat/rhythm games (e.g., Beat Saber) where the player needs precise physical positioning within a fixed area.

rubric.key: Correct comfort, freedom, and genre for teleportation
Correct comfort, freedom, and genre for continuous locomotion
Correct comfort, freedom, and genre for room-scale

questions.question: A VR settings menu has 8pt text on a panel positioned 2 meters from the player. Users report they cannot read the text. Calculate the minimum text height for readability at this distance and explain the rendering technique used for sharp text at variable distances.
questions.answer: The minimum readable text size in VR is approximately 1 degree of visual angle. At 2 meters distance, 1 degree of visual angle corresponds to: height = 2m x tan(1 degree) = 2m x 0.01745 = 0.0349m = approximately 3.5cm. The 8pt text at 2m is far below this threshold, which is why users cannot read it. The text should be at least 3.5cm in world-space height, which is roughly equivalent to 100pt+ at 2m distance on a world-space canvas. The rendering technique for sharp text at variable distances is Signed Distance Field (SDF) rendering, implemented by TextMeshPro in Unity. SDF fonts store the distance to the glyph edge rather than pixel coverage, allowing the GPU to reconstruct sharp edges at any resolution and distance through a smoothstep function in the fragment shader. Unlike bitmap fonts that become blurry when scaled, SDF fonts remain crisp.

rubric.key: Correct calculation of minimum text height at 2m (~3.5cm)
Identifies SDF rendering as the technique for sharp VR text
Explains why SDF maintains sharpness at variable distances

questions.question: Explain the difference between diegetic and spatial UI in VR, and describe when each is the better choice.
questions.answer: Diegetic UI exists as a physical object within the game world that the character and player can see. Examples: a wrist-mounted health display, a physical map the character holds, a computer terminal in the game. Spatial UI is a floating panel positioned in 3D space but not attached to any in-world object. Examples: a settings menu anchored to the player's hand, a floating tooltip near an object. Diegetic UI is better for frequently accessed gameplay information (health, ammo, inventory) in immersive experiences because it maintains the fiction that the player exists in the virtual world. Looking at a wrist watch feels natural. Spatial UI is better for system-level menus (settings, pause, save/load) that are not part of the game world fiction. A floating settings panel is acceptable because settings are inherently meta-game, and trying to embed them in a diegetic object (like a physical computer in every scene) is contrived.

rubric.key: Correctly distinguishes diegetic and spatial UI with examples
Appropriate use case for diegetic UI with reasoning
Appropriate use case for spatial UI with reasoning
