quizzes.topic: Spatial Audio, AR Tracking, and Mixed Reality

questions.question: Explain how HRTF-based spatial audio creates the perception of a sound coming from a specific 3D position using only stereo headphones. Include the three physical phenomena the HRTF models.
questions.answer: HRTF (Head-Related Transfer Function) transforms a mono sound source into a binaural stereo signal that the brain perceives as coming from a specific 3D direction. The three physical phenomena modeled: (1) Interaural Time Difference (ITD): sound arrives at the ear closer to the source slightly before the far ear (up to ~0.7ms difference). The HRTF applies the appropriate delay to each channel. (2) Interaural Level Difference (ILD): the head shadows the far ear, reducing high-frequency content. The HRTF applies frequency-dependent attenuation to the far-ear channel. (3) Pinna filtering: the complex shape of the outer ear (pinna) creates frequency-dependent reflections that vary with the elevation angle of the sound source. The HRTF applies the characteristic spectral coloring for each direction, enabling the brain to distinguish above from below and front from back.

rubric.key: Correct explanation of ITD (time difference between ears)
Correct explanation of ILD (level difference from head shadow)
Correct explanation of pinna filtering (elevation cues)

questions.question: A mixed reality app places a virtual pet on the user's real coffee table. When the user walks around the table, the pet sometimes slides on the surface. Identify two distinct technical causes and describe how to fix each one.
questions.answer: Cause 1: Inaccurate plane detection boundary. The detected horizontal plane may not precisely match the table surface geometry, causing the anchor to shift as the plane estimate is refined. Fix: use spatial anchors rather than relying on the continuously-updated plane position. Once the pet is placed, create a spatial anchor at that position. The anchor uses environmental features to maintain its position independently of ongoing plane refinement. Cause 2: Tracking drift from visual-inertial odometry. As the user walks around, accumulated error in the SLAM system shifts the world coordinate frame slightly. Fix: ensure the environment has sufficient visual features for robust tracking (well-lit room with varied textures), enable relocalization so the runtime can correct drift when it re-observes previously seen features, and snap the pet's vertical position to the scene mesh each frame to compensate for small drift amounts.

rubric.key: Identifies plane detection inaccuracy with valid anchor-based fix
Identifies tracking drift with valid fix involving relocalization

questions.question: Describe two ways that scene mesh occlusion improves mixed reality realism, and explain the rendering technique used to implement it.
questions.answer: Way 1: Virtual objects are hidden when they pass behind real furniture. If a virtual ball rolls behind a real couch, the ball is not visible through the couch, just as a physical ball would be hidden. This prevents the 'X-ray vision' effect where virtual content is always visible regardless of physical obstacles. Way 2: Virtual objects appear to be at the correct depth in the scene. Without occlusion, virtual content always renders on top of the real world, looking like a flat overlay rather than existing in 3D space. With occlusion, the depth relationship between real and virtual objects is correct. Rendering technique: the scene mesh (3D reconstruction of the physical environment) is rendered to the depth buffer but not the color buffer. This means the mesh writes depth values that prevent virtual fragments behind it from passing the depth test, but the mesh itself is invisible because it does not write color. The camera passthrough shows the real surface at those pixels instead.

rubric.key: Two distinct ways occlusion improves realism
Correct depth-buffer rendering technique for invisible occlusion mesh

quizzes.topic: Mixed Reality Design, XR Performance, and Accessibility

questions.question: A VR game targets Quest 3 at 90Hz and must complete all rendering in 11.1ms per frame. The scene currently takes 18ms and is GPU-bound with 320 draw calls and no foveated rendering. Describe a prioritized optimization plan with estimated savings per step.
questions.answer: Prioritized plan: (1) Enable single-pass stereo rendering. Renders both eyes in one pass instead of two, saving CPU draw call overhead and GPU vertex processing. Estimated savings: 4-5ms. (2) Enable fixed foveated rendering at medium level. Reduces fragment shading cost in peripheral zones by 25-35%. Estimated savings: 2-3ms. (3) Reduce draw calls from 320 to under 120 through static batching for non-moving objects and GPU instancing for repeated meshes. Estimated savings: 1-2ms. (4) Compress all textures to ASTC 6x6 to reduce GPU memory bandwidth consumption. Estimated savings: 1ms. Combined estimated savings: 8-11ms, bringing frame time from 18ms to approximately 7-10ms, within the 11.1ms budget. If the result is between 10-11ms with insufficient headroom, additionally enable Application SpaceWarp as a safety net for frame drops during complex moments.

rubric.key: Prioritizes single-pass stereo as the highest-impact optimization
Includes foveated rendering with reasonable savings estimate
Addresses draw call reduction and texture compression
Total savings are realistic and bring frame time within budget

questions.question: Describe three accessibility features for VR experiences and explain how each one also benefits users without disabilities.
questions.answer: (1) Seated play mode with adjustable virtual height: enables wheelchair users and those who cannot stand. Also benefits: travelers on airplanes, office workers during lunch breaks, players who prefer couch comfort, and anyone with temporary injury like a sprained ankle. (2) Spatial subtitle system with directional indicators: enables deaf and hard-of-hearing users to follow dialogue and sound cues. Also benefits: players in noisy environments, non-native language speakers who follow audio better with text support, and players who missed a line of dialogue. (3) Comfort settings with vignetting and snap turn: essential for users with high motion sickness susceptibility. Also benefits: VR newcomers who have not developed tolerance, players returning to VR after a break, and anyone experiencing fatigue during long sessions where sensitivity increases.

rubric.key: Three distinct accessibility features correctly described
Each feature has a valid primary accessibility use case
Each feature has a compelling benefit for non-disabled users

questions.question: Explain what Application SpaceWarp is, how it works technically, and describe one scenario where the visual artifacts it produces are acceptable and one where they are not.
questions.answer: Application SpaceWarp (ASW) allows a VR app to render at half the display refresh rate (e.g., 45fps instead of 90fps) while the runtime generates intermediate frames. Technically: the app renders a frame with color, depth, and motion vectors. The runtime uses these to reproject and synthesize the intermediate frame by warping the color image according to the depth and motion data. This creates the appearance of 90fps from 45fps of actual rendering. Acceptable scenario: a relatively static environment like a museum walkthrough where the user is the primary moving element. The reprojection handles head motion well, and most objects are stationary so motion vector artifacts are minimal. Unacceptable scenario: a fast-paced game with many rapidly moving objects (particles, projectiles, fast-moving characters). ASW produces visible artifacts on these objects: smearing, disocclusion halos (dark edges where previously hidden surfaces become visible), and incorrect motion for objects whose true motion differs from the estimated motion vectors.

rubric.key: Correct technical explanation of ASW with motion vector reprojection
Valid acceptable scenario with reasoning
Valid unacceptable scenario with specific artifact description

quizzes.topic: User Testing, Deployment, and XR Best Practices

questions.question: You are conducting a VR user test and one participant reports feeling 'a little dizzy' after 8 minutes. Describe the correct protocol for handling this situation, including what you should NOT do.
questions.answer: Correct protocol: (1) Immediately offer to pause or end the session. Never pressure a participant to continue through discomfort. (2) If they want to stop, help them remove the headset slowly (sudden removal can cause disorientation). (3) Have them sit down and offer water. (4) Administer the SSQ to record their current symptoms. (5) Note the exact time and activity when symptoms were reported -- this identifies the causal trigger. (6) Allow at least 15-30 minutes of recovery before any further VR exposure. What you should NOT do: (1) Do not tell them to 'push through it' or 'you'll get used to it.' Forcing exposure through discomfort worsens symptoms and can cause lasting VR aversion. (2) Do not invalidate their experience ('the last tester was fine'). (3) Do not let them immediately put the headset back on after a brief pause. (4) Do not record their session as 'normal completion' -- mark it as a comfort-related early termination with the trigger activity noted.

rubric.key: Correct immediate response: offer to stop, assist with headset removal
Records symptom data and trigger activity
Identifies at least two things NOT to do with reasoning

questions.question: Explain the difference between the Simulator Sickness Questionnaire (SSQ) and the System Usability Scale (SUS) in the context of VR testing. Describe what each measures and what score ranges indicate problems.
questions.answer: SSQ measures physical comfort symptoms caused by the VR experience. It has 16 symptoms (general discomfort, fatigue, headache, eye strain, difficulty focusing, nausea, etc.) rated on a 4-point scale (none, slight, moderate, severe). The total severity score and three subscale scores (nausea, oculomotor, disorientation) are calculated. Scores above 15 indicate noticeable discomfort that warrants investigation. Scores above 30 indicate significant discomfort requiring design changes. A score change of 5+ from pre to post-test is meaningful. SUS measures usability -- how easy and satisfying the VR interface is to use. It has 10 statements rated on a 5-point Likert scale. The final score ranges from 0-100. Above 68 is above average. Below 50 indicates serious usability problems. Between 50-68 indicates marginal usability with room for improvement. SSQ and SUS are complementary: an experience can have good usability (high SUS) but cause discomfort (high SSQ), or be comfortable (low SSQ) but confusing to use (low SUS). Both must be acceptable for a successful VR product.

rubric.key: Correctly describes SSQ as measuring physical comfort symptoms
Correctly describes SUS as measuring usability
Provides correct score ranges and their interpretations

questions.question: Name three VR comfort rules that should never be violated, and for each explain the physiological consequence of violating it.
questions.answer: (1) Never override head tracking: if the player moves their head but the virtual camera does not follow, the mismatch between visual and vestibular input is extreme. Consequence: intense nausea and disorientation within seconds, not minutes. This is the single most nauseating thing possible in VR. (2) Never move the camera without player initiation: cutscenes that move the camera, getting pushed by an explosion, or falling without player control create visual motion with no vestibular preparation. Consequence: sudden onset nausea because the vestibular system did not anticipate the motion. The brain interprets this as poisoning (the evolutionary reason for motion sickness). (3) Never change field of view suddenly: abrupt FOV changes (zooming in, transitioning between cameras) create a visual scale shock. Consequence: disorientation and oculomotor discomfort (eye strain, difficulty focusing) as the visual system attempts to recalibrate depth perception and vergence.

rubric.key: Three distinct comfort rules correctly stated
Physiological consequence explained for each rule

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all lab sessions involving VR/AR hardware. Headset time is limited and shared; missing a lab session cannot be made up without prior arrangement. User testing sessions require advance scheduling with participants.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late without prior arrangement. All VR/AR submissions must include a video walkthrough as the instructor may not have immediate headset access for grading.
course_policies.collaboration: Individual assignments must be completed independently. VR headsets may be shared for testing but all code and design work must be original. Peer playtesting is encouraged and should be documented. User testing participants must not be the student themselves.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All code must be original or properly attributed. Use of XR template projects must be disclosed and substantially modified. Submitting unmodified SDK samples as original work will result in course failure. AI-generated code must be disclosed and fully understood by the student.
course_policies.technology_requirements: The department provides Meta Quest headsets for lab use. Students with personal headsets may use them for at-home development. All VR submissions must target Meta Quest 3 as the reference platform. AR submissions may target Quest 3 MR or iOS/Android AR Foundation.
